name: Proxima Centauri
state: red dwarf, stable for trillions of years
fate: will slowly dim over trillions of years — far outliving our Sun
interesting_fact: Has an Earth-sized planet (Proxima b) in its habitable zone. The closest star to our Sun.

name: Alpha Centauri A
fate: will expand into a red giant in ~6 billion years
interesting_fact: Our Sun's nearest neighbor (with Proxima). Part of a triple star system.

name: Sirius
fate: will expand into a red giant in ~660 million years
interesting_fact: Brightest star in Earth's night sky. The ancient Egyptians used its heliacal rising to predict the Nile floods.

name: Epsilon Eridani
state: young main sequence star with a debris disk
fate: long-lived orange dwarf, will outlive our Sun
interesting_fact: One of the nearest stars with a known exoplanet. Target of early SETI searches.

name: Tau Ceti
fate: will expand into a red giant in ~6 billion years
interesting_fact: Has at least 5 exoplanets, two possibly in the habitable zone. Featured in dozens of science fiction stories.

name: Procyon
state: subgiant — beginning to evolve off main sequence
fate: will become a red giant within ~100 million years
interesting_fact: One of the brightest stars in the sky. Has a white dwarf companion — a preview of its own fate.

name: Vega
state: main sequence, rapidly rotating blue-white star
fate: will become a white dwarf in ~1 billion years
interesting_fact: The North Star 12,000 years from now due to axial precession. Carl Sagan's Contact features a signal from Vega.

name: Fomalhaut
state: young main sequence star with a prominent debris disk
fate: relatively short-lived blue-white star
interesting_fact: Surrounded by a vast ring of dust — a possible planet-forming disk. Has the first directly imaged exoplanet candidate.

name: Pollux
fate: will shed its outer layers into a planetary nebula
interesting_fact: The nearest giant star to Earth. Has a confirmed exoplanet — Pollux b — orbiting it.

name: Arcturus
state: red giant, near end of life
fate: will become a planetary nebula and white dwarf within a few million years
interesting_fact: Light from Arcturus was used to open the 1933 World's Fair in Chicago — chosen because it had traveled exactly 40 years since the previous Fair.

name: Altair
fate: will become a white dwarf in ~1 billion years
interesting_fact: Spins so fast (once every 9 hours) that it's visibly flattened at the poles. 20% wider at the equator than at the poles.

name: Capella
state: binary system of two giant stars
fate: both components nearing the end of their giant phase
interesting_fact: Actually a binary pair of giant stars orbiting each other every 104 days. The brightest G-type star system in the sky.

name: Castor
fate: complex evolution of 6 gravitationally bound stars
interesting_fact: What appears as one star is actually 6 stars in 3 binary pairs, all orbiting a common center of gravity.

name: Aldebaran
fate: will become a planetary nebula and white dwarf in a few million years
interesting_fact: Pioneer 10 spacecraft is currently heading toward Aldebaran and will pass near it in about 2 million years.

name: Regulus
fate: will likely become a neutron star in a few hundred million years
interesting_fact: Rotating so fast it's nearly at break-up speed. If it spun just 16% faster, it would fly apart. Only 1 billion years old.

name: Algol
state: eclipsing binary — one star periodically blocks the other
fate: complex binary evolution
interesting_fact: Known as the 'Demon Star' in ancient Arabic. Blinks visibly every 2.87 days as one star eclipses the other — visible to the naked eye.

name: Gacrux
fate: will become a white dwarf
interesting_fact: The northernmost star of the Southern Cross. In the southern hemisphere, it's used like the North Star for navigation.

name: Achernar
fate: will likely become a neutron star
interesting_fact: The most oblate star known — its equatorial diameter is 56% larger than its polar diameter due to extreme spin.

name: Spica
state: binary system, both massive hot stars
fate: both stars will end as supernovae
interesting_fact: Hipparchus used Spica to discover Earth's axial precession in 127 BCE. Both stars will explode as supernovae.

name: Bellatrix
fate: will explode as a supernova in a few million years
interesting_fact: The name means 'Female Warrior' in Latin. J.K. Rowling named a Harry Potter character after this star.

name: Canopus
fate: will likely explode as a supernova
interesting_fact: Second brightest star in the sky. NASA spacecraft use it as a navigational reference point — Canopus trackers are standard on deep space probes.

name: Acrux
fate: will end as supernovae
interesting_fact: The brightest star in the Southern Cross and 13th brightest in the sky. Appears on the flags of Australia, New Zealand, Brazil, and others.

name: Hadar
fate: both will explode as supernovae
interesting_fact: One of the stars forming the 'pointer stars' to the Southern Cross. Its companion is already a subgiant expanding toward explosion.

name: Mira
state: pulsating red giant, dying — brightens and dims over 332 days
fate: will shed its outer layers into a beautiful planetary nebula
interesting_fact: Omicron Ceti — the first variable star discovered (1596). Every 332 days it brightens enough to see with naked eye, then dims again. A stellar heartbeat.

name: Polaris
fate: will explode as a supernova
interesting_fact: The North Star — directly above Earth's north pole. Every civilization in the northern hemisphere has navigated by it for millennia. It will stop being the pole star in ~27,800 CE.

name: Antares
state: red supergiant — on the brink of supernova
fate: could explode as a supernova ANY TIME — possibly has already
interesting_fact: So large that if placed at the center of our Solar System, it would engulf Mars. The supernova explosion may already have happened — we just haven't received the light yet.

name: Saiph
fate: will explode as a supernova
interesting_fact: Despite being similar to Rigel in actual luminosity, it appears dimmer because it radiates more in the UV. One of Orion's knee stars.

name: Betelgeuse
state: massive red supergiant — near death, recently dimmed dramatically in 2019
fate: will explode as a supernova — could happen any moment in astronomical terms
interesting_fact: In late 2019, Betelgeuse dimmed dramatically in what astronomers called 'The Great Dimming' — possibly shedding a mass of material. When it explodes, it will be visible in daylight for weeks.

name: Rigel
state: blue-white supergiant, one of the most luminous stars
fate: will explode as a supernova, possibly within a million years
interesting_fact: 79,000 times more luminous than our Sun. The brightest star in Orion despite being labeled 'Beta'. Named after the Arabic word for 'foot'.

name: Deneb
fate: will explode as a supernova
interesting_fact: One of the most luminous stars known — perhaps 200,000 times the Sun's luminosity. Its exact distance is uncertain between 1,400 and 2,600 light years. A beacon of cosmic uncertainty.

name: Mirfak
fate: will become a red supergiant and eventually explode
interesting_fact: The brightest star in Perseus, which in Greek myth slew Medusa and rescued Andromeda. Mirfak means 'elbow' in Arabic.

name: Alnilam
state: massive blue supergiant — lives fast, dies young
fate: will explode as a supernova
interesting_fact: The central star of Orion's Belt. 375,000 times more luminous than our Sun. Only a few million years old — a stellar infant that will die explosively.

name: Eta Carinae
state: luminous blue variable — one of the most massive and unstable stars known
fate: may explode as a hypernova at any moment — the most dangerous star in our galaxy
interesting_fact: In 1843 it briefly became the second brightest star in the sky, then faded. It's surrounded by the Homunculus Nebula — material it ejected. A future hypernova could threaten life on Earth if we were much closer.

name: UY Scuti
state: red hypergiant — one of the largest known stars in the universe
fate: will explode as a hypernova
interesting_fact: One of the largest known stars — if placed at the center of our Solar System, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter's orbit. The light you see left this star before the Western Roman Empire fell.

name: Zeta Puppis
state: Wolf-Rayet candidate, intensely hot and blowing away its own outer layers
fate: will explode as a supernova, possibly within 1 million years
interesting_fact: One of the hottest and most luminous stars visible to the naked eye. Its stellar wind is so powerful it loses the equivalent of Earth's mass every year.
